draft.subject: the_draft
draft.label: The draft in row nine
draft.blurb: Forty years of maintenance tickets, every one closed 'no fault found'. Somebody should cross-reference them. Nobody ever has.
draft.text: Ninety minutes of cold-spot reports, thermal surveys, and one janitor's increasingly personal margin notes ('it moved my cart AGAIN'). Plotted together, the anomalies aren't random — they trace the shelving rows like someone doing rounds. You learn the trick the janitor never did: where not-quite-to-look. Between one shelf and the next, the air is reading over your shoulder. It puts the file back for you.

plant_7.subject: plant_7
plant_7.label: Plant 7
plant_7.blurb: Three corps, three flagships, one factory stamp. The index lists a file. The file lists a file. Somebody's been here before you.
plant_7.text: Ninety minutes of cross-referencing and the folder is mostly holes — pages withdrawn, 'corrected,' re-filed under weather. What survives: a freight manifest routing Oceania optics, Eurasia chassis plate, and Eastasia lace substrate out of the same unmarked plant, invoiced to the same unlisted shell — Ministry Holdings, no address, no phone. Three enemies, one loading dock. The last surviving page is a staff memo, one line: THE WAR IS THE PRODUCT. Someone has underlined it twice, decades apart, in two different inks. The archive lets you put it back yourself. It feels like being trusted with a knife.
